---
title: "9. Model, Inquiry, Data Strategy, and Answer Strategy"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb03_anatomy_of_design_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** You decide the four parts of your research design and
> confirm they align: a model of the world, one named quantity you want out of it,
> a plan for how the data arrive, and a plan for turning that data into your
> quantity. This chapter equips you to write those four parts and defend that they
> point at the same thing.

## Why this decision matters

Picture a data science lead reading your one-page design before you spend a
semester on it. Her worry is simple: "Show me the exact number you are after, then
show me that the way you collect and crunch the data can actually reach that
number. If those two drift apart, every result you bring me is answering a
question I never asked." A design that skips this step is not rigorous. It is a
hope with good formatting. Getting the four parts to agree, on paper, before any
data exist, is the cheapest quality check you will ever run.

## The concept

Every research design has the same four parts. The course calls them **MIDA**, one
letter each.

- **Model**: your written picture of how the world could work, meaning which
  things exist and what could affect what. Example: "a listener's chance of
  accepting a recommended song runs from 0 to 1, and a new recommender could nudge
  it up."
- **Inquiry**: the one exact quantity you want from that world, named before any
  data arrive. Example: "the average lift in accept rate the new recommender
  causes across listeners."
- **Data strategy**: your plan for how the data come to exist, meaning who gets
  sampled and who gets which condition. Example: "randomly send each session to
  the old or new recommender, then log every accept."
- **Answer strategy**: your plan for turning the data into an estimate of the
  inquiry. Example: "accept rate in the new group minus accept rate in the old
  group."

The four are not a checklist you fill in any order. They must **align**, meaning
your data and answer strategies actually reach the inquiry your model makes
askable. Example of misalignment: a causal inquiry ("the lift the recommender
*causes*") served by a data strategy that never randomizes who sees what can only
show an **association**, meaning two things move together without proof that one
caused the other. Naming the misalignment on paper is the whole job here.

## A worked example

Take a music-streaming app testing whether a new autoplay recommender raises how
often listeners keep the suggested track. Walk the four parts.

The **model** says each listener has two possible accept rates, one under the old
recommender and one under the new, and the new one could be higher, lower, or the
same. The **inquiry** is the average difference between those two rates across all
listeners, one number, causal in kind. The **data strategy** buckets each incoming
session at random into old or new, so nothing about the listener decides which
version they get. The **answer strategy** computes the accept rate in each bucket
and subtracts.

Now audit alignment. The inquiry asks for a caused difference. Random bucketing is
what licenses that word, because it breaks any link between who the listener is and
which version they see. So the four parts agree. Contrast a tempting shortcut:
ship the new recommender to new users and compare them against existing users on
the old one. That data strategy no longer aligns with a causal inquiry, because
new and existing users differ in ways that also move accept rate. The honest claim
collapses from "causes" to "is associated with." Same question, broken alignment.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb03 — The anatomy of a research design: MIDA +
declare → diagnose → redesign**. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work
the cells alongside this chapter. There you build a small simulated world, write
its four MIDA parts, and watch what happens to your answer when a data strategy
quietly stops matching the inquiry.

## Recommended AI prompts

Write your own draft of each part first, then ask. Committing first gives the AI
output something to disagree with.

```
Act as a research-design tutor. Here is my research question: [paste it].
Draft the four MIDA parts as a labeled list — Model, Inquiry, Data strategy,
Answer strategy. State the Inquiry as one exact quantity. Mark each part
"confident" or "guessing." Do not smooth over gaps.
```
After running, verify: read the drafted Inquiry's words next to your question's
words. Do the kind and reach match? This counters **silent scope change**, where
the tool answers an easier, adjacent question than the one you asked.

```
Act as a hostile methodologist. Here are my four MIDA parts: [paste them].
Name the single worst misalignment — a place where my data or answer strategy
cannot reach my stated inquiry. Give one concrete way my design fails, not
praise.
```
After running, verify: check the objection against your own alignment note. This
counters **sycophantic agreement**, where the tool calls a design sound because
you proposed it.

```
Find one real, published study that used a design like mine to answer a similar
question. Give title, authors, year, and venue. If you are not confident it
exists, say so instead of inventing one.
```
After running, verify: open a library database and locate the exact study before
citing it. This counters **confident fabrication**, where the tool returns a
plausible citation for a paper that does not exist.

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
Three choices stay yours. **Which world your question assumes** (the model, and so
what could confound it). **Which single quantity you want** (the inquiry, named in
your words, not the AI's paraphrase). **Whether your four parts align**, and
therefore whether your claim boundary is *causes* or only *is associated with*. An
AI can draft candidate parts. Deciding they agree, and owning the claim that
follows, is research judgment you cannot hand off.
:::

## An AI failure case

You paste your streaming question and ask for MIDA. The tool returns four
confident, well-formatted parts and calls the design "rigorous." Read closely: it
wrote the **data strategy** as "compare new users on the new recommender to
existing users on the old one," and it kept the **inquiry** worded as a caused
lift. That is two failures at once. The data strategy is **plausible-but-wrong**,
because user tenure moves accept rate on its own, and the inquiry wording is a
**silent scope change** from association to cause. You catch it two ways: set the
inquiry's words beside the data strategy and ask whether randomization actually
happened, then sketch the model as a diagram and look for an arrow from "user
tenure" into both the version seen and the accept rate. That open arrow is the
confounder the fluent draft never mentioned.

## Verification laboratory

The key result to verify is the alignment claim itself: that your four parts point
at one quantity. Use **peer reasoning** from the verification guide. Explain your
model, inquiry, data strategy, and answer strategy to a partner and ask them to
name the part that does not reach the others. A second person's first question
usually finds the drift you stopped seeing. For any design whose inquiry uses the
word *causes*, add a second, independent check: draw the **causal diagram** and
confirm no open backdoor path (a common cause of both treatment and outcome) is
left standing. Two independent checks that agree are far stronger than one.

## Project transfer

This chapter is the spine of milestone **M3 — your research charter and MIDA
declaration**. Bring your own project's question and write its four parts, then add
one sentence naming which part you were least sure aligned and why. That alignment
note becomes the claim boundary you defend in your charter, and it is the exact
input chapter 10 builds on when you declare and diagnose the same design.

## Defend your decision

In three or four sentences, state your project's four MIDA parts, name the single
quantity your inquiry targets, and declare your claim boundary: does your data
strategy earn the word *causes*, or does it stop at *is associated with*? Then name
the one part you are least confident aligns, and the uncertainty that would change
your answer if it turned out otherwise. A declaration with no stated boundary and
no stated uncertainty is incomplete.
